title: Unentangled stoquastic Merlin-Arthur proof systems: the power of unentanglement without destructive interference
abstract: Stoquasticity, originating in sign-problem-free physical systems, gives rise to $\sf StoqMA$, introduced by Bravyi, Bessen, and Terhal (2006), a quantum-inspired intermediate class between $\sf MA$ and $\sf AM$. Unentanglement similarly gives rise to ${\sf QMA}(2)$, introduced by Kobayashi, Matsumoto, and Yamakami (CJTCS 2009), which generalizes $\sf QMA$ to two unentangled proofs and still has only the trivial $\sf NEXP$ upper bound. In this work, we initiate a systematic study of the power of unentanglement without destructive interference via ${\sf StoqMA}(2)$, the class of unentangled stoquastic Merlin-Arthur proof systems. Although $\sf StoqMA$ is semi-quantum and may collapse to $\sf MA$, ${\sf StoqMA}(2)$ turns out to be surprisingly powerful. We establish the following results:   - ${\sf NP} \subseteq {\sf StoqMA}(2)$ with $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{n})$-qubit proofs and completeness error $2^{-{\rm polylog}(n)}$. Conversely, ${\sf StoqMA}(2) \subseteq {\sf EXP}$ via the Sum-of-Squares algorithm of Barak, Kelner, and Steurer (STOC 2014); with our lower bound, our refined analysis yields the optimality of this algorithm under ETH.   - ${\sf StoqMA}(2)_1 \subseteq {\sf PSPACE}$, and the containment holds with completeness error $2^{-2^{{\rm poly}(n)}}$.   - ${\sf PreciseStoqMA}(2)$, a variant of ${\sf StoqMA}(2)$ with exponentially small promise gap, cannot achieve perfect completeness unless ${\sf EXP}={\sf NEXP}$. In contrast, ${\sf PreciseStoqMA}$ achieves perfect completeness, since ${\sf PSPACE} \subseteq {\sf PreciseStoqMA}_1$.   - When the completeness error is negligible, ${\sf StoqMA}(k) = {\sf StoqMA}(2)$ for $k\geq 2$.   Our lower bounds are obtained by stoquastizing the short-proof ${\sf QMA}(2)$ protocols via distribution testing techniques. Our upper bounds for the nearly perfect completeness case are proved via our new rectangular closure testing framework.
summary: This paper explores the complexity of quantum proof systems where the witnesses are unentangled but the system is stoquastic (sign-problem-free). It reveals that unentanglement can provide significant computational power even without the destructive interference typically found in general quantum systems.
main_problem: The paper investigates the computational complexity and structural properties of StoqMA(2), a complexity class defined by unentangled (separable) quantum proofs in sign-problem-free (stoquastic) systems.
main_result: The authors establish that StoqMA(2) is surprisingly powerful, containing NP and being contained in EXP, and show that for negligible completeness error, StoqMA(k) collapses to StoqMA(2).
method: The study employs distribution testing techniques to derive lower bounds, a new rectangular closure testing framework for upper bounds, and the Sum-of-Squares (SoS) algorithm for complexity limits.
model_or_system: The model focuses on stoquastic Hamiltonians, which are characterized by real, non-positive off-diagonal matrix elements, and unentangled witness states across multiple registers.
key_observables: Acceptance probabilities of the verifier, completeness and soundness errors, and the presence of destructive interference (or lack thereof).
important_parameters: Completeness and soundness error regimes, number of provers (k), witness length (qubit scale), and the promise gap.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the absence of destructive interference (stoquasticity) and relies on the Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH) for establishing the optimality of certain algorithms.
figures_summary: A summary diagram illustrating the relationships and new inclusions between various complexity classes like NP, MA, QMA(2), and StoqMA(2).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the StoqMA(2) class, establishes lower bounds via distribution testing, provides upper bounds using SoS and rectangular closure testing, and concludes with structural results regarding error reduction and prover collapse.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
